questions.prompt: Which keyword in Python is used to write a test that throws a loud error if a condition is false?
questions.answer_explanation: The assert keyword evaluates a boolean expression and raises an AssertionError if it is False.

questions.prompt: Which git command is used to save a snapshot of staged changes into the local repository history?
questions.answer_explanation: git commit creates a permanent snapshot of the staged changes in the project timeline.

questions.prompt: Which git command is used to place changes in the staging area before committing them?
questions.answer_explanation: git add selects modified files to be included in the next commit snapshot.

questions.prompt: What type of error is raised in Python when an assertion check fails?
questions.answer_explanation: When an expression evaluated by assert is False, Python raises an AssertionError.

questions.prompt: What is the primary characteristic of the Waterfall model in the SDLC?
questions.answer_explanation: The Waterfall model executes SDLC phases in a strict linear sequence, completing each fully before starting the next.

questions.prompt: What is a sprint in Agile software development?
questions.answer_explanation: A sprint is a short, time-boxed development cycle (typically 1-4 weeks) that results in working software.

questions.prompt: What format is commonly used to write a user story in Agile development?
questions.answer_explanation: User stories keep developers focused on users through the template: As a... I want... so that....

questions.prompt: Which SDLC stage is focused on deciding the structure, components, and data model of the application?
questions.answer_explanation: The Design stage specifies how the system components, APIs, and data models will be organized prior to writing code.

questions.prompt: Which term describes a software bug where previously working code breaks due to a new change?
questions.answer_explanation: A regression occurs when an update or addition accidentally breaks existing functionality.

questions.prompt: Why is separating pure logic from input/output (I/O) recommended for code quality?
questions.answer_explanation: By keeping rules separate from print/input statements, you can write clean, automated assert tests for the logic.

questions.prompt: Who is legally and ethically accountable if an AI assistant generates code with a critical bug that ships to users?
questions.answer_explanation: AI is only a draft tool; the human developer who reviews and accepts the code holds ultimate accountability.

questions.prompt: What is the primary role of a code review on a software team?
questions.answer_explanation: Code reviews allow team members to examine proposed changes before merging them to catch bugs and verify quality.

questions.prompt: What file extension is used for Jupyter notebooks, like the ones used in Google Colab?
questions.answer_explanation: Jupyter Notebooks save their JSON structure with the .ipynb extension.

questions.prompt: Which platform is most commonly used to store git repositories online and collaborate on PRs?
questions.answer_explanation: GitHub is the dominant web-based service for hosting Git repositories and conducting pull requests.

questions.prompt: What does a git repository include besides the project's files?
questions.answer_explanation: A repository stores the files alongside the complete, permanent history of all commits.

questions.prompt: Which term describes an isolated timeline in Git used to develop a feature safely?
questions.answer_explanation: Branches allow developers to work on features without affecting the main shipping version.

questions.prompt: What is a pull request (PR) in version control?
questions.answer_explanation: A pull request is a formal proposal to merge code from one branch into another, triggering review.

questions.prompt: Which SDLC stage is concerned with release and hosting of software for real users?
questions.answer_explanation: The Deploy stage publishes the built software to environments (like servers) where users can access it.

questions.prompt: What is the main danger of jumping straight to the Build stage without planning?
questions.answer_explanation: Skipping requirements and design often results in writing code that does not solve the user's actual problem.

questions.prompt: What is the term for a precise, testable statement of what a software system must do?
questions.answer_explanation: A requirement provides a clear, verifiable definition of expected software behavior.

questions.prompt: What is a major advantage of Agile sprints over Waterfall?
questions.answer_explanation: Agile builds software in short feedback loops, allowing early course corrections before changes become too expensive.

questions.prompt: What does a pure logic function do?
questions.answer_explanation: Pure logic functions perform calculations and return values without interacting with the console or external resources.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid Python assert statements that will not raise an AssertionError?
questions.answer_explanation: assert statements pass silently when the expression evaluates to True.

questions.prompt: Which actions move changes along Git's three areas?
questions.answer_explanation: git add stages changes (working to staging), and git commit saves the snapshot (staging to repo).

choices.text: git push to copy local files

questions.prompt: Which are stages of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)?
questions.answer_explanation: Requirements, Design, Build, Test, Deploy, and Maintain are the six SDLC stages.

questions.prompt: Which of the following describe Agile software development?
questions.answer_explanation: Agile uses short feedback-driven loops called sprints rather than finishing stages fully in one long sequence.

choices.text: works in short cycles called sprints
adapts requirements based on user feedback
completes all coding before testing starts
operates as one long linear waterfall pass

questions.prompt: Why do professional software teams use version control systems like Git?
questions.answer_explanation: Version control lets developers recover older versions, collaborate via branches, and audit project history.

choices.text: to recover older versions of files
to work in parallel on branches
to maintain a tamper-evident audit history

questions.prompt: What are the common parts of a Git-based collaboration workflow?
questions.answer_explanation: Collaboration workflows typically utilize feature branches, pull requests, and peer code reviews before merging.

choices.text: compiling files directly to machine binary

questions.prompt: Which of the following are examples of input/output (I/O) operations in Python?
questions.answer_explanation: I/O includes talking to a human (print, input) or writing/reading files from disk.

choices.text: returning an integer value from a function

questions.prompt: Which are potential issues when using code drafted by an AI assistant?
questions.answer_explanation: AI drafts can contain subtle logic errors, hallucinated APIs, and policy/privacy violations.

choices.text: subtle logic bugs that look correct

questions.prompt: What legal mechanism did Richard Stallman use to invent copyleft and enforce software freedom?
questions.answer_explanation: Copyleft uses copyright law to grant users freedoms while legally binding modifications to remain free.

questions.prompt: What is the primary condition of a copyleft license like the GPL?
questions.answer_explanation: Copyleft licenses require that anyone sharing modified versions of the code must also share their source under the same license.

choices.text: code must run on Linux servers

questions.prompt: Which type of software license demands the least from users, usually only requiring attribution?
questions.answer_explanation: Permissive licenses (like MIT or BSD) allow modifications to be closed-source, only requiring attribution.

questions.prompt: Which mathematical tool does Git use to create tamper-evident snap fingerprints?
questions.answer_explanation: Git runs a cryptographic hash function on file and folder contents to produce secure, unique identifiers.

questions.prompt: What type of hash function does Git use to identify files and commits?
questions.answer_explanation: Git traditionally utilizes the SHA-1 hash function to fingerprint repository items.

questions.prompt: What did professor Tim Davis observe that triggered the Copilot lawsuit controversy?
questions.answer_explanation: Davis showed that GitHub Copilot generated blocks of his copyrighted code verbatim, with licenses and attribution removed.

questions.prompt: Which legal doctrine did AI companies cite to defend training models on public code?
questions.answer_explanation: AI model makers argue that training is transformative learning, falling under fair use.

questions.prompt: What term describes when an AI model regenerates training data verbatim?
questions.answer_explanation: When an AI outputs memorized text or code snippets verbatim from training, it is called memorization.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are examples of permissive open-source licenses?
questions.answer_explanation: MIT and BSD licenses are classic examples of permissive licenses.

questions.prompt: How does Git ensure the integrity of a repository's history?
questions.answer_explanation: Git computes hashes from content and links commits by embedding the previous commit's hash, forming an immutable chain.
